findings.rationale: Our property is a specific URL for a RepairProvider, whereas schema.org’s serviceUrl is a generic website to access any service.
The upstream term refers to a certification agency’s URL, which is a more general concept than the repair provider’s specific URL.
Our property specifies a URL for a repair provider, which is a more specific use of the generic website URL defined upstream.
Both properties record the date of the most recent update, though one refers to a DPP instance and the other to a web page.
Our property records the last update of any DPP, whereas the upstream term refers only to updates of a live blog.
Both properties refer to an organization that validates or acknowledges a credential, but one is specifically the verifier while the other denotes recognition of validity.
Our property refers to any material or component name, whereas the upstream property is limited to ingredient or ingredient group names.
The candidate refers specifically to additive or genetic modification names, which is a narrower subset of the broader component/material name concept.
The property specifies the name of a material component, which is a more specific instance of the generic item name defined by schema.org.
The generic component name covers all material or constituent names, while iupacName is a specific chemical naming convention, making the upstream term a narrower special case.
Our property specifies functional units for carbon‑footprint metrics, whereas the upstream property is a general unit of measurement code.
declaredUnit specifies a functional unit for carbon metrics (e.g., kg CO2e/kWh), which is a more specific concept than the generic Measurement Unit defined by UNECE.
The candidate refers to a specific CO₂ emissions unit (g/km), whereas our property covers any functional unit for carbon metrics.
Our Value property accepts any JSON data type, whereas schema.org’s value is limited to values of QuantitativeValue, PropertyValue, etc., making the upstream term a narrower specialization.
The generic value property encompasses any JSON data type, whereas codeValue is a specific textual code used to identify a value.
Our property represents any value of a data element, whereas the candidate is limited to certification standard values.
Our Value property accepts any JSON data type, whereas GS1’s Measurement Value is limited to a numeric value qualified by a unit code.

Our term encompasses all consumer incentives such as refunds, credits, and replacements, whereas schema.org cashBack refers specifically to a cardholder benefit that pays a percentage of expenditures.
Our property describes any consumer incentive (refund, credit, replacement), whereas the upstream property is limited to return fees for customer remorse.
Our property covers all consumer incentives, while schema.org’s incentiveType is limited to tax‑related incentive categories.
Our class represents a specific language‑tagged string, which is a particular type of structured value; thus it is narrower than the general StructuredValue.
Our property specifies a URL for a carbon footprint study, which is a more specific use of the generic website URL defined upstream.
Both properties link to an external file or website, but the candidate is a generic reference while ours specifically denotes a carbon‑footprint study.
The property specifies the issuer of a Digital Product Passport, which is a more specific instance of the generic organization issuing an item defined upstream.
Our property specifies the URI of a compostability certification standard, which is a more specific instance of the generic certification standard defined upstream.
The candidate property covers general consumer usage instructions for a product, while our term specifically addresses safe use instructions for the substance of concern.
Both describe precautions related to handling a product, but our term focuses on safe use of the substance itself while the candidate is specific to preparation or consumption.
Our property provides specific safe‑use instructions for a substance of concern, which is a narrower instance of the broader consumer safety information defined upstream.
Our class covers all material composition information, while the upstream class is limited to textile materials.
Our property specifies units for environmental impact indicators, a more specific use case than the generic measurement unit defined upstream.
Both properties express a unit of measurement for a numeric value, but the impact indicator unit is broader than a dose unit.
Both properties denote a unit of measurement for a numeric value, but the impact indicator unit is broader than a drug unit.

Both properties refer to a URL that identifies an external entity providing verification or certification, but the upstream term is specifically for issuing agencies while ours covers any third‑party assurance.
The SCIP ID is a specific type of identifier, whereas schema.org’s identifier property covers all kinds of identifiers.
Operator Role is a specific type of role within the supply chain, which falls under the broader category of OrganizationRole.
Our class describes a specific piece of a multi‑piece trade item, whereas IndividualProduct is a general single product instance.
LifecycleStage is a specific enumeration of life‑cycle modules, whereas StatusEnumeration is a generic list of status types.
Passport Status is a specific lifecycle token for a DPP, while Certification Status broadly indicates active/inactive certification status.
Our property refers specifically to the lifecycle status of a Digital Product Passport, whereas schema.org’s certificationStatus applies generally to any certification.
The candidate property is a generic measurement of an observation, while our property specifically captures circularity performance data for a product.
Both properties provide repair or maintenance instructions, but the GS1 term focuses on a link to service info while ours is broader repairability information.
Both properties express a duration in years, but our term specifically refers to spare parts availability while the candidate is a general warranty period.
Single-Valued Data Element represents a single point value, whereas QuantitativeValue also allows intervals; thus our class is a narrower specialization.
Our class specifies a single point value, whereas the GS1 Measurement Type also allows intervals; thus our concept is a narrower specialization.
The term describes any single data point, whereas the candidate refers specifically to an item within a data feed.
Single-Valued Data Element specifies a single point value, whereas PropertyValueSpecification is a general specification that can include single or multiple values; our concept is narrower.
The generic single data point concept is broader than the specific nutrition measurement type, which is a particular instance of such an element.
Our property is a general document type, while the candidate refers specifically to medical publication types.
The candidate property is a general identifier for any Thing, while our term specifies an identifier specifically for material components.
Both properties link an earlier instance to a newer one, but the schema.org property is generic for product variants while our term is specific to passport version history.
The Digital Product Passport is a specific type of electronic file, thus it is narrower than the generic schema.org DigitalDocument.
The Digital Product Passport encompasses many product details, including packaging; thus it is a broader concept than the GS1 PackagingDetails class.
Both properties describe a product’s category, but the GS1 property is specific to GPC codes while ours refers to ESPR classification.
Our property specifies the jurisdiction for an EPR registration, a specific type of legislation, whereas the candidate refers to any legislative jurisdiction.
EPR Jurisdiction refers to a country or member state, whereas addressRegion denotes a sub‑national region within a country.
Our property refers specifically to the jurisdiction of an EPR registration, a narrower concept than the general legal jurisdiction described by the candidate.
Indicator Total Value is a specific numeric measurement qualified by unit, whereas Measurement Value is a generic numeric value with unit; our term is a narrower specialization.
Our property is a specific numeric aggregate value of an impact indicator, whereas schema.org’s ‘value’ is a generic property for any quantitative or structured value.
The GS1 ‘Instructions’ property is a general link to any instructions, whereas our property specifically refers to repair instructions and manuals.
The GS1 ‘Service information’ link covers all service or maintenance instructions, while our property specifically targets repair instructions and manuals.
Our class represents a generic ordered list of identical data elements, while the candidate is a specific set of colour code details; thus our term is broader.
Both properties provide a URL to a product‑specific information page, though the specific context (EPREL vs SmartLabel) differs.
Both properties represent the systematic IUPAC chemical name of a substance.
Our property is a general emissions performance container, whereas the candidate refers to a specific CO₂ emission value.
Both properties denote the date after which an item (EPD or generic object) is no longer valid.
Both properties denote the end date of a validity period, though the candidate is used for general items while ours is specific to environmental product declarations.
The candidate property provides general certification information, while OUR property specifically refers to the certification of a data provider.
The OUR property refers specifically to a data provider’s certification, whereas the schema.org property is a general identifier for any certification instance.
The candidate property covers general certification for products, organizations or locations, whereas OUR property is specifically about the certification of a data provider.
The GS1 property is a general certification identifier for any product, party or location, while the OUR property specifically refers to certification of a data provider.
The candidate property refers to any certification subject, while OUR property specifically denotes the certification of a data provider.
The OUR class specifies hazardous substances of concern per SCIP/ESPR, which is a more specific subset of the general chemical substance described by schema.org.
Our class specifies a particular type of hazardous substance (substance of concern) whereas schema.org Substance is a generic matter, making our term a narrower specialization.
Both properties capture the date of completion of an audit or report, but one is for due‑diligence reporting and the other for certification auditing.
Both properties record the name of a standard, but one refers to an EPD reference standard while the other is a general certification standard.
Our class specifies ESPR product categories, which are a specific classification of products; the candidate represents any product, a more general concept.
Both properties provide textual instructions for end‑of‑life handling of a product, but one focuses on dismantling while the other is specifically for consumer recycling.
Both properties record the date an item was issued, though one refers to a digital product passport and the other to a ticket.
Both properties record the date an item was originally issued, though one refers to a digital product passport and the other to a certification.
Both properties denote the date an item (passport or content) ceases to be valid, though the candidate is broader in scope.
Both properties indicate the final date of validity for a document or certification, though one refers to a passport and the other to a generic certification.
The candidate property refers to general sustainability and recycling information, whereas the OUR term specifies a detailed recycled content breakdown.
Both properties denote the media type (MIME format) of a document or file.
Both properties are URLs, but the candidate is a generic website link whereas our property specifically points to authorized repair service providers.
Our property specifically identifies the organization operating a deposit return scheme, whereas the candidate refers to any certified subject.
Performance Information focuses specifically on product performance and durability, whereas Product Information Page is a broader link to general product details.
Our property refers specifically to certifications held by a facility, whereas the candidate property is a general certification attribute for any product, organization or location.
hasCertification is a general property for any entity, while Facility Certifications specifically applies to facilities.
Certification Identification is a generic reference to any certificate, whereas Facility Certifications specifically applies to certificates held by a facility.
Facility Certifications is a specific property for facility-held certificates, whereas Certification Information is a general link to any certification data.
Facility Information covers all manufacturing or processing facilities, while Has Manufacturing Plant refers specifically to a physical plant.
Recyclability Assessment is a specific type of rating applied to product recyclability, whereas Rating is a general evaluation concept.
Registration Number is a specific identifier for an official registration or license, whereas Entry in a register refers to any link to a register entry.
Our property covers any official registration or license number, whereas the candidate refers specifically to a verification number for regulatory records.
Registration Number is a general official identifier, whereas vehicleIdentificationNumber refers to a specific type of registration number for vehicles.
Registration Number is a general official identifier, whereas membershipNumber refers to a specific type of registration number for memberships.
The economic operator responsible for a product includes manufacturers, distributors, importers, etc., whereas ‘Has Manufacturer’ refers only to the producing organization.
Both represent a container of data items, but DataElementCollection holds individual data elements while DataCatalog refers to datasets.
Data Element Collection is a specific container for data elements, while Schema.org Collection is a general collection of items.
The traceabilityPerformance property is a specific metric, whereas measuredProperty is a general property for any measurement.
The candidate property specifies only the maximum energy‑efficiency class, whereas our property can refer to any energy‑efficiency class.
Both properties denote the energy efficiency category/class of a product according to an international standard.
The candidate property specifies only the minimum energy‑efficiency class, whereas our property can refer to any energy‑efficiency class.
The upstream property defines a broader energy‑efficiency category that may include ratings or certifications, whereas the OUR term specifies a specific class within that category.
The upstream property defines a generic numeric measurement value with unit code, whereas the OUR term specifies power consumption in active mode as that measurement.
Both properties denote a version identifier for an object, but the schema.org property is generic to CreativeWork while ours is specific to a Digital Product Passport.
Both properties denote a unique identifier assigned to a formal report or declaration by an issuing organization, though the specific domain (EPD vs general report) differs.
The GS1 term denotes a general regulatory reference number, while our property specifies the registration number for an Environmental Product Declaration, making it a narrower, more specific concept.

The GS1 property describes a generic link to any register entry, while our property specifies the registration number for an Environmental Product Declaration, making it a more specific (narrower) concept.
Both properties provide a URL for returning or taking back a product, but the upstream term focuses on merchant returns while ours specifies consumer-facing take‑back program pages.
The candidate property specifies chemical composition (elements and ratios), which is a narrower, more specific type of material composition.
The candidate property describes textile material composition with percentages, a specific instance of the more general material composition concept represented by our property.
The candidate property specifies a textile material percentage, which is a specific type of material composition that our generic property encompasses.
Our class represents the overall energy‑efficiency declaration of a product, which includes levels and certifications; the candidate is only the enumeration of those levels.
Our class covers all EU energy efficiency classes plus EPREL registration and consumption modes, whereas the schema.org class enumerates only the EU energy efficiency classes.
The candidate class covers all energy‑consumption information for a product, while our class focuses specifically on the declared efficiency levels and certifications.
Our class includes all EU energy efficiency classes, EPREL registration and consumption modes, whereas the schema.org class refers only to EnergyStar certification.
Both properties express a current status as a boolean, but one refers to general compliance while the other specifically concerns certification.
Our class covers overall product performance and durability, while the candidate focuses specifically on energy consumption details, a narrower aspect.
Our property refers specifically to an EPR scheme registration number, whereas the candidate is a general link to any register entry.
Our property is a specific EPR scheme registration number, while the candidate covers any official business registration.
Our property is a specific identifier for a DataElement within its container, whereas the candidate is a generic identifier applicable to any Thing.
Both properties are URLs, but reportUrl is specifically for a due diligence report, whereas referencedFileURL is a generic website reference.
Our property refers specifically to waste prevention information, while the candidate covers broader sustainability and recycling content.
Both properties define the energy efficiency class of a product according to an international standard.
Our property describes any performance class, while the candidate specifies only the maximum energy‑efficiency class.
Our property specifies a performance class rating (e.g., A+++ to G), whereas the candidate covers any energy‑efficiency category, including ratings or certifications.
Our property specifies a Decentralized Identifier (DID), whereas the candidate is a general identifier for any Thing.
Our property specifies a particular recyclability methodology, whereas the candidate is a general link to sustainability and recycling information.
Our class covers only the EU A‑G energy efficiency classes, whereas the schema.org enumeration also includes A+, A++, and A+++. Thus our term is a narrower subset.
Our class specifies the EU Energy Labelling Regulation A‑G classes, whereas the candidate covers a wider range of international energy efficiency levels.
Our class specifies only the EU A‑G energy efficiency classes, whereas EnergyConsumptionDetails is a broader concept covering all information about a product’s energy consumption and efficiency.
Our property describes any hazard impact of a hazardous substance, whereas the schema.org property is limited to serious adverse outcomes in medical therapy.
Our property specifies a URL for a directory of physical collection points, which is a more specific use case than the generic URL property in schema.org.
Our property specifies a URL for a directory of physical collection points, which is a more specific use case than the generic referencedFileURL that can point to any internet resource.
Both properties provide a numeric representation of a stage, but our property is an indicator value for a lifecycle module rather than the ordinal stage number.
Our property is a numeric indicator value for a lifecycle stage, which is a more specific use of the generic measurement value defined in GS1.
Our property specifies a numeric indicator for a lifecycle stage, which is a more specific use of the generic quantitative value concept defined by schema.org.
Our property covers total lifecycle CO₂e, whereas the schema.org property refers specifically to vehicle emissions per km.
The repair portal URL is a specific type of organization page, making it a narrower use than the general GS1 homepage link.
The repair portal URL is a specific type of service information link, making it narrower than the general GS1 Service information property.
Access Rights is a general data‑control concept, while DigitalDocumentPermission refers specifically to file access for a person or group.
Both classes describe deposit‑return arrangements for packaging, but the GS1 term focuses on deposit details rather than the broader scheme participation.
RepairProvider covers all authorised repair providers for any product, whereas AutoRepair is a specific type of repair business limited to cars.
Our class covers all authorised repair providers, while the candidate is a specific type of repair shop for motorcycles.
passportIdentifier is a specific URI for a DPP instance, while schema.org identifier is a general property for any kind of identifier.
tradeItemPieceNumber is a specific sequential index for pieces of a trade item, while schema.org position denotes any item's place in an arbitrary sequence.
Our property includes an ISO 639 language plus a country code, whereas the GS1 property specifies only an ISO 639‑1 language code.
Our property requires an ISO 639 language plus a country code, whereas the GS1 property specifies only an ISO 639‑1 language code.
Both properties use IETF BCP 47 language tags (ISO 639 + region), but schema.org inLanguage refers to content or action, whereas our property is a language tag of an arbitrary value.
Both use IETF BCP 47 language tags, but our property is a generic language tag for any value, whereas schema.org’s knowsLanguage applies specifically to persons or organizations.
Both refer to product instructions, but the GS1 property covers general usage while our term is specifically for end‑of‑life handling.
Both properties refer to consumer recycling guidance, but our property is a URL to instructions whereas GS1’s property holds the textual instructions themselves.
The GS1 sustainabilityInfo property links to general sustainability and recycling information, whereas our end‑of‑life instructions specifically provide a URL for end‑of‑life handling.
Our class refers to any external document, while the candidate is a specific type of reference for APIs.

Our DocumentReference encompasses any external document (reports, manuals, guides) in multiple languages and formats, whereas ReferencedFileDetails is a narrower class that specifically provides URL and file‑specific information.
DocumentReference covers any external document, while NoteDigitalDocument is a specific type of digital note.
Impact Indicator Result is a specialized quantitative value that includes an indicator type, unit, and aggregated value, whereas QuantitativeValue is a generic point or interval measurement.
Both properties indicate the specific version of a schema that an instance conforms to, using a string or URL.
Both properties record a version identifier, but the DPP Schema Version is specific to DigitalProductPassport instances, whereas schema.org’s version applies generally to any CreativeWork.
HazardousSubstance is a specific type of chemical substance that meets EU CLP criteria, making it narrower than the general ChemicalSubstance.
Both properties provide a human‑readable description of an item, but our term is specific to trade item pieces while schema.org’s description is generic.
tradeItemPieceDescription is a general human‑readable description of any trade item piece, while textileMaterialDescription refers specifically to the material within a product.
tradeItemPieceDescription refers to a description of an individual trade item piece, which is a more specific concept than the general product description provided by Product Description.
Both properties express the role of an economic operator or organization, though the upstream term uses a generic party/organization context.
Both properties denote the monetary deposit amount for a returnable package, expressed as a quantitative value with currency.
Both properties refer to substances considered regulatory concerns present in a product, though our definition specifies database requirements and explicit presence.
Both properties record a date related to compliance activities, but one records verification while the other records audit completion.
Both properties denote a date related to certification or compliance, but one specifies the start of validity while the other records when verification occurred.
Both properties record the date of a compliance-related audit or verification, but one specifies a general compliance check while the other explicitly references a certification audit.
Both properties denote the date on which a document or ticket is issued.
Our property applies to any document’s issue date, whereas the candidate is limited to legislation adoption dates.
The issue date of a document is a general concept, while the initial certification date refers specifically to the issuance of a certification.
Our property applies to any document’s issue date, whereas the candidate refers only to the audit completion date for certification.
The candidate property describes any government‑assigned organization ID, whereas the OUR term specifies a single EU‑wide economic operator identifier.
The candidate property represents any organization ID governed by an administrator, while the OUR term specifies a particular EU‑wide economic operator identifier.
Both describe a time span measured in years, but one refers to product technical life while the other specifies warranty duration.
The candidate property defines a generic unit code, whereas the OUR term specifies a technical lifetime value that uses such a unit.
The OUR property covers multiple industry classification systems (ISIC, NACE, NAICS), whereas the candidate property refers only to NAICS.
Our property covers multiple industry classification schemes (ISIC, NACE, NAICS), whereas the candidate is specific to ISIC v4.
Both properties record a date/time when data was updated or published, but the candidate refers specifically to structured‑data generation rather than a generic DPP data update.
Both properties record the date of the most recent review or update of content, though one refers to DPP data and the other to a web page.
Both properties are numeric ratings, but the candidate is a general content rating while ours specifically measures recyclability.
Both properties provide a URL for redeeming an item, but the deposit redemption channel is specific to container returns while the offer redemption URL targets online offers.
Our property specifies a return link for deposit redemption channels, which is a more specific instance of the generic merchantReturnLink defined in schema.org.
Our property specifies a URL for a document, while the GS1 property refers to any internet resource; thus our term is a narrower specialization.
Both properties provide a URL to an external file or webpage, but the GS1 term is specifically tied to product information whereas our term is a generic document reference.
Our property applies to any document URL, whereas the GS1 property is a specific subtype for certification agency URLs.
Our property specifies a URL that points to a document reference, whereas schema.org’s url is a general link for any item.
Our property is a generic URL for any document, whereas the GS1 term refers specifically to URLs linking to company filing information.
Our property points to a specific URL for separate collection requirements, which is a subset of the broader sustainability and recycling information link.
Our property refers to a specific URL for separate collection requirements, which is a narrower subset of the broader sustainability and recycling information link.
Our class covers facility identification, location, and certification, while the candidate focuses only on linking identifiers to locations.
Facility Information is a specific type of organization (manufacturing/processing facility) identified by GLN, whereas Organization covers all entities.
Our class represents a specific type of place (manufacturing or processing facility) with additional attributes, making it narrower than the general schema.org Place.
Facility Information is a specific type of Place, thus it is narrower than the generic Place class.
Both properties represent the ISO 639‑1 language code for a digital asset, with identical scope and definition.
Compostability is a specific type of certification, whereas Certification is a general class for any official attestation.
Our property specifies the exact regulation a due‑diligence statement refers to, whereas the GS1 property is a general link to any applicable regulatory information.
Operator Information specifies economic operator roles (manufacturer, importer, distributor) identified by GLN, which is a more specific subset of the broader GS1 Organization concept.
Operator Information is a specialized class of economic operators (manufacturers, importers, distributors), which are specific types of Organization.
Repair Provider is a specific type of service provider, thus it is narrower than the general schema.org provider.

The depositReturnScheme property refers to a product’s participation in a return‑deposit scheme, whereas hasReturnablePackageDepositDetails specifies the refund amounts for that package; thus our term is a broader concept.
Deposit-Return Scheme is a specific type of recycling scheme that includes deposit, whereas Packaging Recycling Scheme covers all recycling schemes.

depositReturnScheme refers to the overall scheme a product participates in, while returnablePackageDepositRegion specifies only the geographic region of that deposit; thus our term is a broader concept.
Both represent a collection of codes that define a classification system, though CategoryCodeSet is generic while CustomsCommodityCodeType is specific to customs codes.
Both properties represent the length of a manufacturer’s warranty, using the same unit codes.
Our term specifies a classification system for customs commodity codes, whereas the upstream Type Code is a generic code list.
Recycled Content is a specific aspect of Packaging details, making it a narrower subclass.
The identityCredentialUrl property specifies a URL for a verifiable credential, whereas referencedFileURL is a generic website link; the former is a narrower, more specific use of the latter.
Both represent an element within a collection, but the OEC dataElement is specifically a member of DigitalProductPassport structures, whereas schema.org’s dataFeedElement is a generic feed item.
The OEC energyEfficiency property is a general concept, while schema.org’s energyEfficiencyScaleMax specifies only the maximum class on a particular scale.
The OEC energyEfficiency property is a general concept of energy efficiency, whereas hasEnergyEfficiencyCategory specifies a particular category or rating within an international standard.
Both properties refer to the energy efficiency category of a product, but our term is generic while the candidate specifies it as part of consumption details.
EPREL Registration Number is a specific type of register entry, whereas registryEntry refers to any generic register entry.
Our EPREL Registration Number is a specific type of regulatory verification number used for EU energy labelling, whereas the candidate term is a general regulatory verification number.
EPREL Registration Number is a specific type of regulatory reference number, whereas regulatoryReferenceNumber covers any record in any regulatory system.
The candidate property refers to general sustainability and recycling information, whereas our term specifically links a product to its recyclability assessment.
Both properties are URLs for compliance verification, but the candidate refers to any certification agency while ours is specifically for EPR scheme operator verification.
oec:expectedLifespan expects a gs1:QuantitativeValue (value plus unit), whereas the GS1 value property is a generic numeric measurement; thus our term is more specific.
